owner_information.owners: Daniel R. McAllister and Karen L. McAllister, as joint tenants

property_and_request_details.legal_description_of_property:
A tract of land located in the NE 1/4 of the SE 1/4 of Section 14, Township 4 South, Range 73 West of the 6th P.M., Clear Creek County, Colorado, being more particularly described as follows:

Lot 7 and the southerly one-half (S 1/2) of Lot 8, Block 3, Ponderosa Ridge Subdivision, according to the Plat thereof recorded in Book 6 at Page 42 of the Clear Creek County Records, County of Clear Creek, State of Colorado; together with that portion of vacated Pine Cone Drive adjoining said lots as described in Quitclaim Deed recorded under Reception No. 2014-003158 in the records of Clear Creek County, State of Colorado.
property_and_request_details.acreage_of_each_current_lots:
Lot 7: 0.92 acres (as shown on recorded subdivision plat)
Southerly one-half of Lot 8: 0.46 acres (based on survey by High Peak Surveying, Inc., dated 2016-09-12)
property_and_request_details.acreage_of_each_proposed_lots: Proposed Lot A: 1.38 acres total (consolidation of existing Lot 7 and the southerly one-half of Lot 8, adjusted for right-of-way and easements per attached survey)
property_and_request_details.describe_reason_for_request:
We are requesting an exemption to legalize a prior division and subsequent recombination of land that occurred without formal County subdivision approval, and to bring our single homesite into full compliance with Clear Creek County regulations.

Background: When we purchased the property in 2015, the title commitment and seller’s disclosures identified two distinct tax parcels associated with what appeared to be a single homesite: all of Lot 7 and the southerly half of Lot 8, Block 3, Ponderosa Ridge Subdivision. The existing single-family residence, detached garage, well, and septic system were all constructed across the interior line between Lot 7 and the southern half of Lot 8. At the time, we were first-time rural property buyers and relied heavily on our realtor and the title company. We understood that the two tax parcels functioned as one legal lot because they had historically been conveyed together since the late 1980s, and our lender underwrote the loan as one residential property.

After moving in, we applied for various permits (a small addition in 2017 and a deck replacement in 2019). During a recent conversation with the Planning Department in 2024 regarding a potential accessory dwelling unit, staff reviewed our file and informed us that the way the property had been divided and reconfigured in the past may not have complied with the County’s subdivision regulations. Specifically, it appears that an earlier owner informally split Lot 8 and conveyed only the southerly half to be combined in use with Lot 7, without going through a formal subdivision or exemption process. While the Assessor’s records and our deed reflect the current configuration, the Planning Department records do not show an approved subdivision or exemption legalizing this configuration as a single lot for building purposes.

Reason for request: Our primary goal is to correct this historic irregularity so that the land configuration supporting our existing home is recognized as a single, legal lot of record. We are not trying to create additional development potential or increase density. Instead, we want to:

1. Ensure that the existing house, septic system, and garage are all located entirely within a single legal lot that meets current County standards to the greatest extent possible;
2. Remove any ambiguity for future building permits, including a potential small accessory dwelling unit above the detached garage for our aging parents;
3. Avoid future title and lending complications if we refinance or eventually sell the property; and
4. Resolve any potential non-compliance associated with the earlier, informal division of Lot 8.

Existing improvements and occupancy: The property is improved with a single-family residence originally constructed in 1983, a detached two-car garage built around 1990, a permitted on-site wastewater treatment system (septic) last inspected in 2021, and a domestic well. We have been living in the home full-time since 2015. During our recent due diligence for the accessory dwelling unit inquiry, Planning staff advised us that the existing home does not have a clear, current County occupancy approval record that aligns with the current combined-lot configuration, likely because of the historic lot-line issues described above. For this reason, we are applying under the exemption category for an improved property without proper County occupancy approval.

Practical impacts of the current situation: The current, irregular legal status of the lots creates several problems for us:

- Building permits: When we met with a planner this spring, they indicated that any new permits for structural changes, an accessory dwelling unit, or even a larger deck could be delayed or denied until the lot configuration is legalized.
- Financing and title: Our mortgage lender has already asked for clarification on the legal description during a recent rate modification. The title company flagged the split Lot 8 issue as a potential exception that could complicate future transactions, particularly if a buyer’s lender requires a clear legal lot of record.
- Estate planning: We are in the process of setting up a living trust and want the property title and legal description to be straightforward for our children. Leaving them with a property that has an unresolved illegal division of land would make administration of our estate more complicated and potentially more expensive.

Requested action: We are asking the County to approve this exemption to:

- Recognize the combination of Lot 7 and the southerly half of Lot 8 into one legal lot, to be identified as “Proposed Lot A” on the attached survey by High Peak Surveying, Inc.;
- Legalize the historic division of Lot 8 and the existing configuration that has been used as a single residential parcel for several decades; and
- Confirm that our existing residence and associated improvements are located on a single, legally recognized lot of record for zoning and building purposes.

We have attached a current improvement location certificate, a full boundary survey showing the proposed single lot, copies of the prior deeds back to 1980, and a narrative from the surveyor explaining how the current parcel evolved. We are also willing to record any plat, deed, or other documentation required by the County to memorialize the approved exemption. Our intent is simply to bring the property fully into compliance, protect our long-term investment, and make sure that any future owners have a clear and accurate understanding of the lot’s legal status.

In summary, this request is about correcting a past, unintended violation of the subdivision regulations so that one existing, long-established homesite can continue to be used and modestly improved in a lawful and transparent way, without creating any new lots, additional residences, or increased development density in the area.
